# Gaussian regression: jaw length ~ body length

Now we add a **predictor**. Larger fish tend to have larger jaws — let's model
jaw length as a linear function of body length.

## The model

$$
\text{jaw}_i \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_i,\ \sigma), \qquad
\mu_i = \alpha + \beta\,\text{body}_i
$$

In FANGS:

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    jaw[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], sigma)
    mu[i] <- alpha + beta * body[i]
  }
  alpha ~ dnorm(0, 50)
  beta  ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  sigma ~ dunif(0, 50)
}
```

- $\alpha$ — intercept (jaw length at body length 0; often not meaningful, but
  needed).
- $\beta$ — **slope**: extra jaw length per unit body length. This is usually the
  scientific quantity of interest.
- FANGS rescales predictors internally for efficient sampling and back-transforms
  for you, so you can interpret $\beta$ on the original scale.

## Reading the result

Check the **Predictions** tab: it overlays the posterior mean regression line and
its 95% band on the data. The **Summary** tab gives the posterior for $\beta$ —
if its 95% CI excludes 0, body length credibly predicts jaw length.

## Your task

Fit this model to `jaw-length.csv` in FANGS, then enter your posterior means for
$\alpha$ and $\beta$. From here on, the challenge is simply: **fit it correctly
and check your answer.**
